critique.summary: Kalpa is an atomic and transactional Linux desktop environment based on the Plasma Desktop from the KDE Project. It is derived from openSUSE Tumbleweed and MicroOS, and is a member of the openSUSE Project.
mainPoints: Kalpa offers a Plasma Desktop Environment from the KDE Project.
The desktop is derived from openSUSE Tumbleweed.
The base system is derived from openSUSE MicroOS.
Kalpa is a member of the openSUSE Project.
Installation media and documentation are available.
Community engagement is facilitated through Matrix Chat, Mastodon, openSUSE Forums, and OpenBuildService.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Kalpa being an atomic and transactional Linux desktop using the KDE Plasma Desktop, derived from openSUSE Tumbleweed and MicroOS, and being part of the openSUSE Project are consistent with available information from openSUSE community sources and related Linux projects. The references to community channels and documentation are typical for open-source projects. However, the article lacks citations or external references to verify these claims independently.
considerations: The article does not provide detailed technical specifications or comparisons with other similar Linux distributions.
There is no information about the project's maturity, user base, or development status.
No external sources or third-party reviews are cited to support the claims.
The article does not discuss potential limitations or challenges of using Kalpa.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the usability, motivation, and practical experience of using OpenSUSE Kalpa and similar atomic Linux distributions. Users share mixed impressions, with some appreciating the immutability and update model, while others question the benefits over traditional distros and highlight usability challenges.

comments.author: miggol
comments.text:
Been running Aeon, the Gnome version of Kalpa, on my personal laptop for about six months now. I came from Tumbleweed so the learning curve wasn't steep. Overall the experience has been good!

The one major issue I had from the start was non-free Bluetooth codecs like AptX. That required me to taint the base image and add a non-official repo. It was messy but that was mostly down to it being a learning process, if I had to do it again I could probably do it with a single run of `transactional-update shell`.

The installer is super minimal and surprisingly user-friendly. One thing I remember is that there was zero partitioning choice: just use the full disk for encrypted btrfs and you get no swap (but zram swap is on by default). If you use OpenSUSE with secure boot enabled (as intended) then hibernate is prevented by `kernel_lockdown` anyway.

Snapper by default is nice, but you also get that with Tumbleweed. I ran into no applications that I couldn't get from Flatpaks or export from a distrobox, the latter being mostly for obscure stuff I need to compile myself. And my main toolbox hosts my Emacs environment that I spend most of my time in besides Firefox.

It's hard to recommend a MicroOS desktop over Tumbleweed, the latter being a great all-purpose distro as it is. But I'm hoping the benefits of forcing this "rootless" paradigm on myself will appear when it's time to move to a new machine. Just copy over my home directory and distroboxes and I'm golden, I could even switch to ARM without hesitation.

The distroboxes help with migrating because if I want to compile a newer version of that obscure program from earlier, I don't have to hunt down all the arcane requirements again. They're all still there waiting for me, in a Fedora/Ubuntu/Arch/whatever distrobox, depending on what works best for that program. At least that's the theory.

Happy to answer questions.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed firsthand account of using a related atomic Linux distro, highlighting practical issues and benefits, making it highly informative and credible.

comments.author: drnick1
comments.text: I am not sure I fully understand the usability trade-offs when it comes to these "atomic" distros. One the one hand, security seems to improve markedly, since the  root filesystem is largely immutable. On the other hand, it does seem that a lot of straightforward things become harder. I generally dislike flatpaks and favor a low-level, bare-metal approach to things and atomic distros seem to go against that. Maybe I should just run some experiments in a VM.
comments.analysis: The comment thoughtfully weighs security benefits against usability drawbacks, reflecting a balanced and accurate perspective on atomic distros.

comments.author: stryan
comments.text: Kalpa is great and hits way above its alpha status; I've been running it on my laptop for months now with zero issues. It's been really nice to not have to worry about updates, just gotta reboot it every now and then and most things just work.
comments.analysis: A positive user experience report that supports the viability of Kalpa despite its early development stage, adding useful insight.

comments.author: quantummagic
comments.text:
This web page doesn't do a good job of motivating the reader.

I understand what the Plasma Desktop Environment is. But what is "atomic and transactional Linux"? What are the advantages to the alternatives? What other projects are similar? What is the motivation for this project in particular? Most importantly, why should I want to use it?
comments.analysis: Raises valid concerns about the clarity and motivation behind the project, pointing out gaps in the presented information.

comments.author: raphinou
comments.text: I've installed https://getaurora.dev/en/, another atomic Linux distro, for a non technical user and find it really good. I've read arguments that its architecture was better than kalpa, but I don't find it back and I have no sufficient knowledge or experience of both to have an opinion.
comments.analysis: Offers a relevant comparison to another atomic distro but admits limited expertise, making the comment cautiously credible.

comments.text: This is a cool idea, but it’s not clear what problem it’s solving. Tumbleweed is already great
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about the necessity of Kalpa given existing solutions, a reasonable viewpoint though lacking detail.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text: Isn't OpenSUSE for sale? At the least distrowatch said this recently.
comments.analysis: Mentions a recent rumor or news about OpenSUSE's ownership, which may be relevant but is not elaborated or confirmed.

comments.author: gethly
comments.text: since flatpacks/snaps/appimages are containerized-ish, i see no point in these immutable distros any more. also, cosmic is where the focus of linux desktop should be, not kde or gnome.
comments.analysis: Dismisses immutable distros based on containerization but conflates unrelated desktop environment preferences, reducing credibility.

comments.author: butILoveLife
comments.text:
Has anyone had a good long term experience with Atomic?

I admittedly only used it on a 13 year old gaming computer and couldn't get the GPU drivers because... you know containers.

This is something trivial with a regular install. (Especially with LLMs to assist)

I want to like Atomic, but it feels like an Apple-like regression in computing.
comments.analysis: Shares a negative personal experience highlighting hardware compatibility issues, a valid concern though somewhat anecdotal.

comments.author: zokier
comments.text: This blog post might provide useful context: https://sfalken.tech/posts/2024-06-08-how-do-aeon-and-kalpa-...
comments.analysis: Provides a helpful external resource for further understanding, adding value to the discussion.
